data.title: Chart Of The Day: What'll It Take To Get These Commodity Stocks Moving?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Mining shares have been on fire all year long. But what will it take to get a different batch of commodity stocks moving? I’m talking, of course, about energy names.

Take a look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day. It shows the year-to-date performance of the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE), First Trust Natural Gas ETF (FCG), SPDR Oil & Gas Exploration & Production ETF (XOP), and the VanEck Oil Services ETF (OIH).

XLE, FCG, XOP, OIH (YTD % Change)

(Data by YCharts)

While there is some overlap in holdings, each ETF focuses on different names and subsectors in the crude oil and natural gas businesses. XLE is heavily into mega-cap, multinational, diversified energy names, while FCG holds more gas-heavy producers and transportation companies. Yet, the performance overall is similar: lackluster. It ranges from -6% for OIH to +4.7% for XLE.

What’s the culprit? Concern that too much oil supply is hitting the market at a time when demand growth remains tepid.

This year, OPEC+ members have largely rescinded output cuts that totaled 2.2 million barrels per day (BPD). Overall global supply is expected to grow another 1.9 million BPD next year, according to the International Energy Agency. Meanwhile, global consumption is expected to rise just 700,000 BPD to 104.4 million BPD in 2026.

In short, the supply-demand picture is much cloudier in the energy space than it is in the precious metals market. But as the saying goes, the best cure for low prices is... low prices. We’ll likely see drilling and production activity cool if oil and gas prices remain subdued. That, in turn, will eventually boost investor interest in the sector.

So, if you’re looking for something that hasn’t run yet in the resources sector... or that might offer compelling value... you might want to check out some of the energy funds mentioned here.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: Alibaba's Big Comeback: Why Q1 Results Weren't A 'Miss'
data.description:
Double miss for Alibaba Group Holding Limited (NYSE:BABA) (OTCPK:BABAF), with Q1 2026 EPS at $2.06 ($0.10 less than expected) and revenues reaching $34.57 billion ($910 million less than forecast).

At first glance, these results may seem terrible, but I will show you how this fiscal Q1 was actually very positive overall. The market shares this opinion, and the stock is jumping 3% in pre-market trading.

Q1 2025 Highlights

To better understand why this quarterly report was a success, I think a little context is needed.

Under the leadership of Eddie Wu (September 2023), Alibaba has begun a gradual process of reducing waste and focusing on its core businesses. The Alibaba of the past was a highly diversified giant, perhaps too much so, as it was losing its competitive advantage in its core segments; too many unnecessary investments and, above all, low-margin and low-growth businesses.

With Eddie Wu, the tune has changed, and Alibaba is selling some of its historic but low-potential businesses quarter after quarter. Among the most important are Sun Art, sold for $1.70 billion, and Intime, sold for $1.02 billion. These businesses were no longer attractive to management, but their sale clearly had an impact on financial statements. Although low-margin, they were still capable of generating billions of dollars in revenue.

If we look at Q1 revenue growth, including Sun Art and Intime, it would only be 2%. However, excluding them from the calculation (which I believe is more accurate), YoY revenue growth rises to 10%. So Alibaba is back to double-digit growth.

Don't give too much weight to the collapse in cash from operations, as it is heavily impacted by multiple disposals. Furthermore, free cash flow is strongly negative as CapEx has more than tripled YoY. Alibaba is investing heavily in AI services and recently also in its core business, China Commerce.

While the divestment strategy is limiting sales growth on paper, it is leading to a significant improvement in the cost structure.

Cost of revenue and administrative expenses significantly reduced their weight relative to revenue, while product development and marketing expenses increased.

That's what happened in general. Now let's take a look at how each individual operating segment performed in Q1 FY2026.

China E-commerce

China E-commerce remains Alibaba's most important segment and has finally returned to double-digit growth. Revenues increased by 10% YoY, thanks in particular to quick commerce and China commerce wholesale, up 12% and 13%, respectively.

Adjusted EBITA fell by 21% YoY, and although this may seem like a bad result at first glance, it is not. It mainly depends on the significant investments made in Taobao Instant Commerce, Alibaba's service that allows users to buy online and receive the product in a few hours.

By leveraging Cainiao's logistics chain, Alibaba can offer an almost instant e-commerce service that users are really enjoying. Just think that in the first three weeks of August, active users on Taobao increased by 25% and 88VIP members (users who spend the most) grew by double digits to over 53 million.

The Taobao Instant Commerce service will require significant investment, but its potential is huge. Consider that users can also purchase food through it and have it delivered directly to their homes in a short time. This could see Alibaba dethrone Meituan, China's leading food delivery company.

International Digital Commerce Group

International commerce is a high-growth segment and includes platforms such as AliExpress, Trendyol, and Lazada.

Sales grew by 20% YoY, but the biggest news concerns a marked improvement in operational efficiency. In fact, adjusted EBITA is close to completely eliminating the loss.

In addition to selling products themselves, Alibaba is making the most of other ways to monetize through its apps, with advertising a key part of this strategy:

Our international wholesale platform saw broader adoption by merchants of its AI-powered tools for marketing, procurement and product listing, which provided multiple ways for the platform to monetize.

Cloud Intelligence

Alibaba's Cloud is not its most important business, but it is certainly the one with the highest potential.

Revenue growth continues to accelerate and has reached 26% YoY. Adjusted EBITA grew at the same pace, which is perhaps the only flaw, as I expected a greater improvement in profitability. After all, we are talking about a highly scalable business, and EBIT tends to grow faster than revenue.

In any case, I remain satisfied with the overall performance, and management is focusing heavily on the Cloud in terms of CapEx. Demand for AI services is insane and has grown by triple digits for the eighth quarter in a row:

AI-related product revenue maintained triple-digit year-over-year growth for the eighth consecutive quarter. As AI demand continues to grow rapidly, we are also seeing increased demand of compute, storage and other public cloud services to support AI adoption. We will continue to invest in anticipation of customer growth and technology innovation, including AI products and services, to increase cloud adoption for AI and maintain our market leadership.

With such strong demand, the billions invested are almost certain to yield a solid return. In fact, I believe Alibaba's main problem is that it has invested too little in its cloud infrastructure in the past. Now it needs to catch up and grow its cloud revenues by more than 30% if not more.

All Others

All Others is the segment that includes the remaining businesses, and there is no particular news in this regard. Freshippo, Amap, Hujing DME, and Alibaba Health achieved operational improvements, but the sale of Sun Art and Intime weighed on the final result. Unlike in previous quarterly reports, the company also included Cainiao in this segment; its results were probably negative once again and were no longer relevant enough to mention separately.

Although it generates a large amount of revenue, All Others is Alibaba's weakest segment, but the positive aspect is that management is aware of this. Over time, it is reducing its weight, and I expect further divestments in the future.

Conclusion

Overall, I consider this quarterly report to be positive, and as a shareholder, I am very satisfied with it. I wanted a greater focus on the core businesses, and that is what we got; I wanted Alibaba to return to double-digit growth, and it succeeded (excluding Sun Art and Intime); I wanted the Cloud to exceed a growth rate of +20%, and it succeeded.

For the next quarter, I expect Cloud to grow above 30% (AI services are starting to weigh heavily on its revenues) and, above all, for China Commerce to continue to expand at double-digit rates. On the International side, I would be very pleased if it finally generated positive EBITA. Further divestments of inefficient businesses are welcome, especially if the profits are reinvested in Cloud.

As for buybacks, the company has slowed down the purchase of its own shares.

Not everyone may agree with this decision, but I do. There are too many important investment opportunities, and it would be a waste not to take advantage of them just to reduce the number of shares in circulation. Furthermore, the price per share is no longer $80, and this affects the effectiveness of the buyback.

Either way, I still consider Alibaba to be significantly undervalued; a company like this cannot have a forward P/E ratio of only 14x. At the very least, it has the potential to increase EPS by 10-15% over the next 5-10 years, and this estimate is probably too conservative. If it were a U.S. company, it would have a forward P/E ratio of at least 20-25x, in line with the major American AI-focused tech companies. Up to $150 per share, it remains a strong buy in my opinion.

data.title: Yellow Cake: Analysis Of Uranium Supply And Demand
data.description:
Introduction

I usually tend to avoid analyzing or investing in companies linked to raw materials; however, I would like to make an exception with this article. Recently, I have become interested in the world behind nuclear energy production, delving into the entire uranium supply chain and the main companies operating in it. I would therefore like to use the analysis of Yellow Cake plc (YLLXF) to explore these issues in greater depth and understand whether investing in uranium is an opportunity.

Business Model

Yellow Cake's business model is very simple: the company purchases and holds long-term stocks of uranium (U₃O₈). The purchase of uranium is financed by the sale of shares, which usually takes place when the share price is equal to or higher than the NAV. The company has an agreement to purchase uranium worth up to $100 million per year from Kazatomprom (the world's leading uranium producer) until the end of 2027. The stocks are ultimately held in Cameco and Orano's storage facilities in Canada and France, respectively.

An investment in Yellow Cake offers direct exposure to uranium price trends. The main ETFs that invest in the uranium sector do so by purchasing companies involved in the production chain, such as mining companies or companies that develop technologies related to this sector. If you want direct exposure to uranium price trends, Yellow Cake is the best option, as investing in companies that extract it also exposes investors to exploration risk and other operational risks typical of a mining company. However, it should also be noted that Yellow Cake does not generate any cash flow for its shareholders, and that investing in companies that extract a specific raw material offers leveraged exposure to the price of that raw material, which may be appreciated by some investors.

Another aspect that needs to be addressed is management compensation policy and Yellow Cake's "TER", or the ratio of operating expenses to book value. In the first image, you can see the five main uranium-related ETFs, with an average TER of 0.67%. For comparison, in the second image, I have created a table showing the trend in Yellow Cake's operating expenses and equity from 2019 to 2025, with an average of 0.92%; we can therefore say that the management cost is higher than that of ETFs.

At this point, I believe it is appropriate to analyze the company's Long Term Incentive Plan [LTIP] to understand whether the management incentive metrics are reasonable or not. First, it should be noted that only the CEO and CFO have access to this incentive plan and receive free options with a vesting period of three years. The allocation of options is subject to two criteria:

  • Performance of Yellow Cake's share price relative to a basket of comparables (80% of valuation):
  • comparison with Sprott Physical Uranium Trust (15%),
  • b. comparison with the spot price of U3O8 uranium (15%),
  • c. comparison with a basket of 3 uranium ETFs (50%).

Relative performance determines the vesting level (100%, 50%, or none).

  • Material growth in uranium inventories and group revenues (20% of the assessment).

The three ETFs used for comparison are: Global X Uranium ETF (URA), Sprott Uranium Miners ETF (URNM), and Sprott Junior Uranium Miners ETF (URNJ).

The terms of the incentive plan seem reasonable to me and, even though the cost is higher than that of ETFs, I believe that the risk of pay-without-performance is reduced, given that managers' compensation is linked to a benchmark that is consistent and appropriate to the company's business.

That said, in order to understand whether or not to invest in Yellow Cake, it is necessary to understand the uranium market and the factors that drive the supply and demand for this resource, given that the value of this company is exclusively linked to the value of its uranium reserves.

Uranium Supply

As you can imagine, uranium production is an extremely critical and internationally controlled activity, and although uranium is a globally distributed resource, actual production is heavily concentrated in a few countries and depends largely on political and economic decisions. At every level of the supply chain, the market is controlled by a few companies, the main ones with state participation.

Kazakhstan is the world's leading producer of uranium (43% of global production), followed by Canada (15%) and Namibia (12%). The mining companies that dominate the market are therefore Kazatomprom (Kazakhstan), Cameco (Canada), and Orano (France, a group active in the entire fuel cycle and a producer accounting for 9-11% of global production). Kazakhstan is also the country with the largest uranium reserves and the lowest extraction costs.

After the long pause in nuclear development following the Fukushima accident, uranium production began to recover in 2023, reaching 54.500 tU, an increase of 12% compared to 2020 levels. Following the improvement in market conditions, some mines undergoing maintenance were reactivated to ensure supply to the growing fleet of nuclear power plants globally. Recent uncertainties affecting the market include the Nigerian authorities' revocation in July 2023 of Orano's permit to exploit the Imouraren deposit, followed in June 2024 by the revocation of GaviEx Uranium (Canada-based) for the Madaouela project, creating significant uncertainty regarding future production centers.

Leaving aside these unforeseen events, many producers have expanded production in recent years. At the same time, governments are developing new frameworks in countries that previously had no uranium production. It should be noted that opening or expanding a uranium mine is not a simple undertaking and requires a lot of time, with many variables that can slow down the launch of these projects.

All investor presentations by uranium-related companies state that there is a gap between production and demand, which will lead to an increase in the price of uranium. However, as indicated by the Nuclear Energy Agency (NEA), this gap between production and demand has existed since 1990 and has been consistently filled by secondary supplies, primarily consisting of reprocessed fuel, stockpiles, and disarmament materials from nuclear weapons programs. The NEA report highlights that although this gap has begun to close in recent years, projections indicate that it could widen again, given the expected increase in demand in the coming years. Although the presence of inventories, including military stockpiles, will continue to represent an element of uncertainty for future investments, reactor requirements will increasingly need to be met by primary production.

In its latest report (Uranium 2024: Resources, Production, and Demand), the NEA identifies two possible scenarios:

  • Low-production scenario: If all existing and committed mines operate at or near their stated production capacity, low demand is expected to be met until 2031. However, for high demand, a production shortfall is anticipated to emerge around 2027.
  • High-production scenario: If planned and prospective production capabilities are included, high demand could be met by primary production until 2037. In this scenario, low demand would be satisfied until 2042.

Uranium Demand

As of January 1, 2023, there were 438 commercial nuclear reactors in operation worldwide and 58 under construction. A significant increase in the number of nuclear reactors is expected in the coming decades, which are essential for producing a stable, low-cost energy source and reducing emissions. In 2022, the European Commission included nuclear energy in its EU taxonomy, classifying it as an environmentally sustainable investment that will help decarbonize the European Union's economy. The countries investing most heavily in nuclear power are India and China. Alongside these developments are the US, which remains the largest user of reactors; France, which is heavily dependent on nuclear power and has launched strategies to maintain and renew its existing fleet; and Japan, which is gradually bringing back into operation the reactors shut down after Fukushima.

In the short-term, the quantities of uranium required for reactor operation can be predicted with a high degree of certainty. Generally, higher uranium prices have encouraged utilities to reduce uranium requirements by specifying lower tails assays at enrichment facilities, to the extent possible. In recognition of these market trends, uranium requirements for the operational lifetime of projected new reactors have been reduced from 175 tU/GWe/yr to 160 tU/GWe/yr (from a 0.30% to a 0.25% tails assay). When determining the quantities of uranium that will be needed, it should also be taken into account that first load fuel requirements are around 60% higher than reloads for plants in operation. In addition, the excellent performance of currently operating reactors has led to a trend toward extending their useful life. The tables below show the NEA's estimates for installed capacity and the quantities of uranium needed to meet it until 2050. In 2050, installed capacity is expected to be 2.3 times that of 2021, and the uranium needed is expected to be about 2.4 times that of 2022.

One factor that introduces uncertainty into these forecasts is undoubtedly possible technological developments, such as the future adoption of fourth-generation reactors, which promise to be able to use waste from old power plants as fuel. However, the reactors currently under construction use “traditional” technologies that have been in use for decades and are known to be reliable.

Conclusion

Yellow Cake is an excellent way to invest in uranium in a “pure” way, without exposure to the operational risks typical of mining companies. In light of the outlook for uranium supply and demand in the coming years, it is reasonable to assume a structural increase in the price of this metal, positively influencing the value of Yellow Cake's reserves and its NAV. However, given the absence of cash flows, it is extremely difficult to value the company, and in my opinion, this does not make it a good option for a classic long-term investment.

Another risk to consider is that it is unclear what will happen after the contract with Kazatomprom expires at the end of 2027. Yellow Cake may find it difficult to negotiate a new supply agreement, meaning that the company's uranium reserves could remain stable. Clearly, this does not reduce the value of the company, but the mechanism adopted so far of issuing shares when their price is above NAV in order to buy more uranium could be difficult to continue.

In conclusion, considering current reserves, the GBP/USD exchange rate, the price of uranium, and Yellow Cake's share price at the time of writing, the share price is trading at a discount of approximately 9% to NAV.

data.title: PDI: I Remain Bullish About This Income Fund
data.description:
Since I started covering the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (NYSE:PDI), there has been a bear case discussing the liquidity of PDI’s distribution and that they wouldn’t be able to maintain it throughout an inflationary environment. PDI has ridden out the storm, is up 2.04% over the past year, and has paid a monthly distribution of $0.23 since September 2015. When I look for income-producing assets I am on the hunt for quality income that comes with a strong long-term investment case. PIMCO is a global leader in active fixed income, and it is much easier to gain exposure to these markets through PDI and let their management team do the work. I believe that shares of PDI will continue to generate large amounts of recurring income and as the macroeconomic environment shifts PDI’s underlying assets should experience significant appreciation as bond prices should increase as yields fall. Another positive is that since PDI is a leveraged fund it should benefit from improved borrowing costs as the Fed lowers rates. Many investors have been waiting a long time for Fed Chair Powell to pivot and in the latest speech from Jackson Hole I believe we received the signal that the Fed will shift on monetary policy in September. As the risk-free rate of return starts to decline again allocating capital toward risk-free assets such as money markets and CDs will look less appealing and PDI’s distribution yield of 13.61% will look very enticing. I am long PDI, and reinvesting the monthly income has allowed me to organically build out a stronger income stream each month.

Following up on my previous article about PDI

Since my previous article about PDI (can be read here) shares have appreciated by 5.3% while the S&P 500 has increased by 11.31%. When the monthly distributions are accounted for PDI’s total return over this period has been 9.01%. I had discussed why PDI’s 14% yield was still attractive despite its share price declines. I felt that PDI’s focus on agency mortgages and dynamic asset allocation provided a level of resilience and that its actively managed fixed-income portfolio is well-positioned for a rebound. PDI continued to deliver large amounts of income on a monthly basis, and I am more bullish today than I previously was. We are one step closer to rate cuts now that Fed Chair Powell delivered his address from Jackson Hole and I am following up with a new article to discuss why I feel PDI is going to rally into the end of the year and continue pumping out large amounts of income.

Risks to investing in PDI

While I am bullish on PDI, there are several risk factors that investors should consider. PDI is a leveraged CEF, which makes it sensitive to interest rates, credit events, and macroeconomic shifts. If, for some reason, something occurs in the data to make the Fed change their stance on monetary policy, it could be very negative for PDI as it could lead to declines in bond prices and the underlying NAV. PDI employs a leverage ratio that exceeds 30% and in a higher-rate environment the chances of its underlying holdings experiencing higher rates of defaults or credit downgrades increase significantly. If companies in their portfolio experience hardships, then PDI’s distribution could face coverage shortfalls as some of its assets could turn illiquid and disrupt the strong distribution yield. Investors should do their own due diligence as it’s difficult to research PDI’s underlying assets and a lot of faith is being placed on the management team. Investors should also be aware that PDI focuses on fixed income, and its share price is unlikely to replicate the returns of an S&P 500 index fund.

Fed Chair Powell just signaled that rate cuts are coming in my opinion and that is bullish for PDI

In Fed Chair Powell's speech from 8/22, he addressed the economic situation and the near-term outlook for monetary policy. Fed Chair Powell stated that “with policy in restrictive territory, the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance.” Throughout the address he discussed the current economic situation and the near-term outlook for monetary policy, but all roads led back to the idea that adjusting their policy stance may be necessary. This was enough for CME Group to update the chances of a rate cut at the September meeting to 86.2% while getting 2 rate cuts by the end of the year increased to 48.3% and getting 3 rate cuts increased to 37.3%. Looking out to the end of 2026, the most likely scenario is that rates would fall 225 bps from where they are today which is super bullish for PDI. This would create a night and day difference in the macroeconomic environment which would have drastic implications for fixed income.

If we do get a prolonged Fed cutting cycle, I think that we’re going to see PDI’s share price get back to the mid-20s. PDI predominantly invests in global fixed income securities which include high-yield bonds, mortgage-backed securities (MBS), emerging market debt, and other credit instruments. When interest rates decline the prices of existing fixed income securities typically increase because their higher coupon payments become more attractive relative to newly issued bonds with lower yields. PDI’s portfolio includes a mix of investment-grade and below investment-grade bonds that would benefit from the inverse relationship between interest rates and bond prices. An ongoing cutting cycle like the one CME Group is projecting would likely result in a sustained bond rally that would increase PDI’s NAV over time. PDI’s portfolio also has interest rate-sensitive assets such as MBS and corporate bonds, and a lower rate environment would reduce the discount rates used to value future cash flow.

PDI has a large allocation toward bonds which are debt securities that pay a fixed interest rate over their term. The yield on a bond is the return an investor will earn through its payments, which is calculated as the annual coupon payment divided by the bond's current market price. When interest rates fall the yield on newly issued bonds decreases because they are priced to reflect the lower rate environment. PDI’s portfolio of existing bonds will continue paying their higher coupon rates which makes them more attractive to investors compared to new bonds with lower yields. The inverse relationship will cause older bonds that traded at a discount to increase in value as investors buy them until their effective yield correlates to newly issued bonds. I believe that this will take PDI’s share price into the mid $20 range over the next 6-12 months. When I look at PDI’s share performance, there is a direct correlation to a higher rate environment and PDI’s share price falling. I am expecting a breakout to occur as rates fall under 4% to the upside.

PDI’s distribution yield is very lucrative and much more effective than traditional income funds

I believe that PIMCO has built a better income machine through PDI than many investment vehicles on the market. PDI went IPO for $25 in 2012, and since then it has produced $41.62 of distribution income which is 166.47% of its share price. Not only have investors received their entire investment back, but they have also generated an additional $16.62 per share in distributions while the shares are still producing $2.65 of annualized income. This is where my income-focused investing style comes into play because I am not concerned about a -22.08% drop overtime in the share price considering that the investment has already been paid for from the income for long-term investors. It’s hard to get upset about a fluctuating share price that is spending a few years under its IPO price when the main investment objective is to generate recurring distribution income. PDI has never cut the distribution and provided investors with several raises over the years. I still think this is a great opportunity to lock in a 13.61% distribution yield, as I believe PDI’s share price will increase as the Fed lowers rates.

There is less reason from an income perspective to invest in an ETF such as the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF Shares (VYM) that is yielding 2.54% with an average 5-year dividend growth rate of 3.59%. If I were to put $100,000 into VYM, I could purchase 719.22 shares. If I held it for 6 years, I would generate $2,538.84 of dividend income in year 1 and the dividend would grow to $4.21, which is an increase of 19.29% ($0.68). The annual dividend would increase to $3,023.69, and over the 6-year period of the $100,000 investment there would be $16,645.10 of dividend income generated. PDI, on the other hand, would generate $13,603.70 of distribution income off a $100,000 investment in the first year and if the distribution doesn’t increase over the 6-year period PDI would generate $81,622.18 in distributions. I think there is less reason to purchase lower-yielding equity-based ETFs because from an income perspective, the dividend growth from VYM doesn’t correlate to enough income generated to get excited. I think that as the Fed lowers rates, we’re going to see a lot of capital move into funds like PDI because of their income-generating potential and I am adding to my position before the share price gets back into the $20s.

Conclusion

Fed Chair Powell has spoken, and he clearly indicated that adjusting their policy stance is on the table due to the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks. This is very bullish for PDI because most of its underlying assets will greatly benefit causing their NAV to increase and pushing PDI’s share price higher. I think that shares of PDI have the potential to get back into the mid $20s as the Fed takes its rate slower while maintaining their current distribution rate. From an income perspective, there is less reason to look at low single-digit yielding ETFs for income compared to a fund like PDI that is comprised of global fixed income assets with double-digit yields. I think that PDI can generate large amounts of recurring income and large monthly distributable income for its shareholders, and I am very bullish heading into the remainder of 2025.

data.title: Free Cash Flow As A Proxy For Quality In Europe: Introducing The S&P Europe Quality FCF Aristocrats Index
data.description:
By Elizabeth Bebb

The S&P Europe Quality FCF Aristocrats® Index introduces the quality free cash flow (FCF) methodology to the European region. The index methodology selects high quality companies in the S&P Europe LargeMidCap universe that emphasize consistent and efficient free cash flow generation.1 Our recent education paper explores the use of FCF metrics in creating indices with quality factor characteristics.

As with the existing S&P Quality FCF Aristocrats Indices, the new European index starts by selecting those companies that have 10 years of positive FCF generation, including positive FCF for the last 12 months. The constituents are then selected using the FCF score metrics, FCF ROIC and FCF margin, and weighted by float market cap multiplied by FCF score.

Over the long term back-tested period, the index outperformed its benchmark by 150 bps since April 30, 2006, while also demonstrating lower volatility with reduced drawdowns. The historical capture ratio also demonstrates the index’s upside potential with defensiveness against downside risk.

Over the period studied, in the majority of cases when the market saw large drawdowns, the S&P Europe Quality FCF Aristocrats Index saw a lower drawdown than the benchmark. The most noticeable of these was during the onset of COVID-19, when the index only matched approximately 50% of the market fall realized by the benchmark.

As of the most recent rebalance, the index had higher tilts toward the U.K., Switzerland, Denmark and the Netherlands compared to the benchmark, which has more weight in Germany and Southern European markets.

Active sector weights are shown in Exhibit 5. Historically, there were higher allocations on average to Health Care, Consumer Staples, Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology, with a significant tilt away from Financials and Energy.

More recently, the weights in Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology increased significantly, showing higher weights compared to the benchmark. Meanwhile, the weights to the Consumer Staples and Health Care sectors decreased. Underweights in Financials2 and Energy remained significant.

The Magnificent 7 stocks have been the topic of much discussion for their impact on the S&P 500. In Europe, the 11 companies that have led market performance are known as the “Granolas.” The index has representation in almost all these companies (excluding Nestlé) and has more than double the weight of the underlying benchmark, recognizing the high FCF scores and quality of these companies over the long term (see Exhibit 6).

The S&P Europe Quality FCF Aristocrats Index is the newest addition to the S&P Aristocrats Index Suite. This regional index has demonstrated a strong quality tilt across its back-tested history, providing a benchmark that uses FCF as proxy for long-term quality in the European market.

1 The S&P 500® Quality FCF Aristocrats Index and S&P Developed Quality FCF Aristocrats Index were both launched in December 2024.

2 Some Financials stocks are excluded due to the availability of metrics (see methodology). All Real Estate sector constituents are excluded.

data.title: Verizon: Stable, Undervalued, And Offering High Yield
data.description:
Verizon (NYSE:VZ) is an American technology and communications behemoth offering a compelling 6.12% dividend yield. High-quality, mature business is still growing, suggesting it is a relatively safe choice for investors who favor stable dividend income.

With 20 years of consecutive dividend growth and 24 years of consecutive dividend payouts, while trading at a forward P/E of 9.4, nearly a 35% discount to peers, Verizon appears to be an attractive entry point for long-term investors. The communications giant is a great diversification option, as its yield offers a defensive approach that benefits investors during turbulent times. The broader market has rallied significantly since the April lows without a meaningful pullback, indicating it may be time for a breather.

I think Verizon is a Buy with a $49 price target, suggesting a 10% upside. I think the company will perform in line with the benchmark, but its 6% dividend yield will offset the extra risk. I think having a defensive play in the portfolio is a great way for a diversified approach—it may not grow at double digits, but during turbulent times it offers limited downside.

FQ2 2025 Supports the Bullish Stance

On July 21, Verizon reported strong FQ2 2025, supporting the bullish narrative. Total revenue for the quarter was $34.5 billion, indicating a strong 5.2% increase year-over-year. The company's adjusted EPS grew as well, with a $1.22 quarterly result, representing an increase from $1.15 reported the same quarter last year (6% growth).

Moreover, the company reported 4.1% year-over-year adjusted EBITDA growth with $12.8 billion from $12.3 billion last year. Verizon has indicated that free cash flow came in at $8.8 billion over the first 6 months of the year, compared to $8.5 billion a year ago, about a 3.5% increase. The company is no longer a double-digit growth story, but recent results highlight its maturity and stability.

The company also highlighted its wireless service segment increased by about 2.2% on a year-over-year basis, with a $20.9 billion. Verizon added 300,000 new customers across its mobility and broadband platforms, with a fourth consecutive quarter of Core Prepaid subscribers. Investors should be satisfied with the company's results, signaling it has surpassed 5 million subscribers for their Fixed Wireless Access—a development reassuring investors.

It was a strong quarterly performance for Verizon, supported by raised full-year guidance. The company expects bottom-line growth of 2% at the midpoint, with cash flow from operations to come in at $38 billion at the midpoint. The company continues to execute and assures investors that its fundamentals remain intact.

Undervalued Compared to Peers With Strong Profitability Metrics

I think Verizon presents an attractive opportunity for long-term investors, as they may benefit from both the company trading at undervalued levels and a stable dividend yield. I believe this duo setup is ideal for dividend investors who favor purchasing a high-yielding stock at an enticing price.

Verizon was previously a cheaper stock from a forward P/E perspective, but with recent results and promising guidance, it should be trading at a premium. Despite a forward P/E elevated compared to its recent years, it is about 35% lower than the peers' average at 14.58. This suggests that Verizon is undervalued and there is room for upside.

On a PEG GAAP (TTM) basis, the company is valued at 0.17 compared to the sector median of 0.45. The overall communication segment appears to be undervalued from a PEG perspective, but a nearly 62% lower ratio supports our bullish stance for Verizon.

The following valuation metrics also suggest that Verizon is well-positioned for upside:

Metrics Verizon Sector Median
EV/EBITDA (FWD) 7.14 8.63
EV/EBIT(FWD) 11.26 16.69
Price/Book (FWD) 1.75 2.04
Price/Cash Flow (FWD) 4.99 8.17

From a valuation standpoint, the communication behemoth appears undervalued versus the industry's average. EV/EBIT on a forward at 11.26, while the average company trades at 16.69, indicates larger undervaluation, while other metrics favor Verizon by 15% to 30%. I think this may represent one of the largest valuation disconnects, considering Verizon is a dominant name in the industry, has a mature and stable business model, and offers a substantial yield to its shareholders via quarterly dividend.

Heavy-leverage Business, but Lower Interest Rates Should Reduce Risks

On both EV/Sales TTM and FWD basis, at 2.62 and 2.60, respectively, the communication giant is valued higher than its competitors, but it is mainly driven due to its significant obligations at $174.74 billion. Verizon is a high-leverage business, which is concerning.

At this point, its total obligations are almost equal to its market capitalization of $186 billion, which offsets some of the bullish narrative and supports lower valuations.

Looking at Verizon's balance sheet, long-term debt is a significant risk and concern for its business. Over the past few years, the company has reduced about 15% of its long-term debt from $143 billion in 2023 to $124 billion as of the most recent report. However, this remains a substantial amount, and maintaining it through a high-interest environment may be challenging.

Verizon's income statement indicates that last year the company spent $6.6 billion in interest expense. This was an increase of almost 23% compared to 2023. It is concerning and slightly surprising, considering that Verizon paid down around $17 billion of its long-term debt throughout the period, but its interest expenses increased dramatically.

I think that Verizon will benefit significantly from a lower interest rate environment, as it will be able to refinance at more favorable conditions. Such a large amount paid in interest expense represents about 3.5% of the market cap annually ($6.6 billion versus $186 billion). Reducing its leverage could offer substantial upside for investors.

Another important thing to consider is that markets are forward-looking. Despite being a heavy-leverage business, if the company progresses, the market may start pricing it early. That is why I believe it is an attractive entry point to consider for long-term investors, realizing the high-interest rate environment won't last forever.

Despite Market-average Growth, Profitability Metrics Indicate Outperformance

Verizon is past its rapid growth phase—it is a slowly but steadily growing business that is highly predictable with a defensive approach. The communication company's revenue grew 2.05% year-over-year, slightly below the industry's average of 3.36%. Its EBITDA grew 4.05% over the past 12 months, while competitors recorded a 6.45% increase.

To offset, Verizon's EBIT growth rate is expected at 13.18%, compared to peers' nearly 18% lower growth rate of 11.19%. It is a mixed bag, mainly due to its liability-heavy balance sheet and different business stages versus the competition.

Remarkably, Verizon's bottom line grew significantly—over 61% in the past 12 months, nearly 3x higher than the industry's average. That is also one of the reasons why the company is trading at a lower price-to-earnings ratio than the average business in the landscape. Return on equity grew by a significant 52% compared to peers' 14%, roughly 4x higher.

The profitability metrics signal Verizon outperformance. For instance, gross profit margin on a TTM basis is 59.48% versus peers' at 53.71%—about a 10% advantage. Verizon operates at nearly 3x greater operating margin (TTM) at 13.28% compared to the industry's average at 4.52%. This contributes to a significant cash from operations (TTM) of $37.10 billion, versus only $275 million reported by peers.

Other profitability metrics also signal Verizon outperformance versus the sector median:

Metrics Verizon Sector Median
Return on Common Equity (TTM) 18.26% 6.25%
Return on Total Capital (TTM) 7.15% 4.41%
Return on Total Assets (TTM) 4.75% 2.07%
CapEx/Sales (TTM) 12.39% 3.35%

Despite its slower-than-market growth and heavy leveraged capital structure, Verizon appears appealing from a fundamental analysis perspective. I think a high dividend yield makes it more attractive for long-term investors.

Dividend History and High Yield Rate Support the Upside

The company's dividend history is phenomenal—Verizon has consistently grown and paid its dividends to shareholders for over 20 years. To put it in perspective, the average company in the landscape has an 11-year track record with only one year of dividend growth. Shareholders value Verizon's dividend history—and due to its stability, it could be trading at a higher premium.

The dividend yield itself is exceptional. The average company in the industry offers 3.48%, thus Verizon's over 6% dividend yield is compelling for investors. The growth rate of the yield is not impressive at 1.88% on a TTM basis, but to be fair, I don't think investors would appreciate a higher increase, given its heavily leveraged capital structure. As it improves, Verizon could potentially increase its already high dividend yield further.

The cash dividend payout ratio at 56.45% versus the industry's average of 29.46% indicates elevated risk to future growth and the continuation of dividend payments. However, I think management understands and prioritizes a shareholder-friendly dividend approach. I believe it values its dividend history and would assume its continuation is its priority. Despite its challenges and debt-heavy balance sheet, it is a highly profitable business that operates at higher than market-average margins, supporting a dividend security stance. Net income rose significantly from $11.6 billion in 2023 to $17.5 billion in 2024, regardless of higher interest expense.

I think that Verizon will continue being an impressive dividend company, cherishing its history and building fundamentals for continued success.

It is a mature business that is highly profitable with an attractive dividend yield. Considering its debt-heavy financial structure, it will take time for fundamentals to improve, but at its current valuations, it appears as an interesting opportunity to consider for long-term investors. Its defensive approach, given its stability and predictability, may be a great offset to a growth-heavy portfolio, offering diversification.

Key Risks and Concerns

There are risks and concerns related to Verizon that may significantly weigh on its stock price in the short term.

Being debt-heavy with a significant interest rate burden, it remains a key concern for the following years. Despite management's progress to pay down its debt, over $6 billion in interest expenses are a significant addition to debt payments. If the Fed decides on higher-for-longer, it may affect the company's expectation to grow its bottom line, which could result in multiple contractions.

Verizon is a well-known brand and a dominant company in the communications landscape. However, it faces strong competition from T-Mobile and cable operators, such as Comcast, which may threaten Verizon's margins. Competitive pricing pressures may affect profitability, stressing the stock price.

I think that the postpaid phone subscriber decline of 9,000 in Q2 is slightly concerning. If the decline persists, it may indicate that Verizon is losing market share to its main competitors, AT&T and T-Mobile. I think this is the segment to follow and monitor—if Verizon reports net addition next quarter, it would indicate this was a minor bump and the red flag would fade.

Management execution is another focus area for investors. Verizon has raised its guidance, but a miss would significantly impact investor sentiment. On the other hand, if management exceeds its forecasts, Verizon could see a significant upside.

Conclusion: Verizon Should Perform in Line With the Broader Market, but 6% Yield Makes It Attractive

I think the current undervaluation and high dividend yield represent an attractive entry point to consider building a position in Verizon for long-term investors who favor dividend stocks. Investors may benefit from portfolio diversification with a well-known, defensive name. Verizon is a high-quality, mature business that operates at strong margins and is highly profitable.

Its dividend history and the consecutive growth rate are phenomenal traits of the business. Its net income indicates that, despite a debt-heavy capital structure and high interest expenses, it is likely to maintain its shareholder-friendly, high-yield dividend strategy. The risk may be elevated, but I don't think investors should be concerned about whether they would pause dividend growth or payout.

I believe Verizon is a Buy with a $49 price target, suggesting about a 12% increase. It would be in line with the average performance of the broader market, but the upside comes from a high dividend yield. I think Verizon is a great company to consider for investors who favor diversification and its defensive approach. With markets rallying without a significant pullback since April, it may be due for a pause, and Verizon should be an alternative to consider for investors.

26 Wall Street analysts suggest a $48.74 price target over the next 12 months, projecting 11.13% upside. This is in line with my expectations, as the stock is currently undervalued compared to peers, and a high dividend yield suggests the company should be trading at a premium. Verizon is a highly profitable business that supports its defensive approach and continued dividend growth.

data.title: Leonardo: Long-Term Winner With Strong Prospects Ahead
data.description:
Back in December 2022, I rated Leonardo S.p.a (FINMF)(OTCPK:FINMY) a Strong Buy (Leonardo: Significantly Undervalued But Poised To Grow). My thesis was clear: inflationary pressures warned the investors about profit margins, and they likely oversold the company, making it a value bargain. Secondly, already 3 and a half years ago, the company secured a big slice of defense contracts both in Europe and the US, focusing on more nice products than competitors, making it a “safe” investment. And finally, the clever acquisitions made to boost the growth, put the company in a good spot to further grow in the future.

In the meantime, I also wrote two other articles (Leonardo: A Great Long-Term Pick and Leonardo: Double-Digit Growth Since My First 'Strong Buy,' But There's Still Room To Go), indicating how Leonardo was pivotal for the rearmament of Europe in a growing period of uncertainty. After 3 and a half years, the company, which I recommended at $8.10, has yielded +600%.

The thesis of this article is clear: the idea I had is still valid today. Leonardo, not only proved to be among the world’s most important defense companies but showcased great capabilities in foreseeing the future needs of Europe. Therefore, Leonardo is still a company where to invest.

ReArm Europe

First, one of the major growth drivers of Leonardo is and will be ReArm Europe, a strategic initiative carried out by the European Union, which has the objective of boosting EU defense capabilities by 2030, investing a total of €800 billion. This plan comes in response to the Russian aggression in Ukraine and basically serves as a deterrent against future Russian aggression on European soil. Furthermore, the EU needs to quickly boost production on armored vehicles, drones, and ammunition, which proved to be crucial in high intensity warfare in Ukraine.

It is reasonable to conclude that Leonardo will receive a substantial amount of these funds since it is one of the major European defense firms. Moreover, ReArm Europe will be primarily dedicated to EU and Ukraine-based industries. This limits the possibility of giving away investments to the US-based companies, while boosting European defense production.

Drones Production and Future of Warfare

Another lesson from Ukraine regards the use of drones. Cheap and easy-to-produce FPV (first-person view) drones proved to be very effective against expensive and hard-to-replace armored vehicles. Moreover, UAVs (unmanned aerial vehicles) demonstrated a good cost-effectiveness ratio, especially against costly air defense systems. These advances in warfare quickly affected industries in the last year, since many analysts recognized their usefulness in future warfare. Leonardo, has already positioned itself in the industry by announcing a joint venture with Baykar, a Turkish company leader in the production of unmanned systems.

According to the June 2025 press release,

the new company, LBA Systems, will be based in Italy

(Source: Leonardo Press Release)

and will be a key player in Europe. Furthermore, Leonardo already possesses capabilities and experience in cutting-edge electronic systems, thus providing added value to the products of the joint venture.

Iveco Defence Acquisition

As for ground warfare, Leonardo didn’t emerge as a major player. In this sense, Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF)(OTCPK:RNMBY) proved to have an advantage in Europe, since it is leading armored vehicle production. In my opinion, this should not be viewed as a problem for the competitiveness of Leonardo, since the company has always been more electronic-warfare/navy/air-force-oriented, following the needs of Italian defense.

However, Leonardo quickly caught up. Back in October 2024, it established a joint venture with Rheinmetall for European tank production. More recently, the company has announced the acquisition of Iveco Defence (Iveco Group: (OTCPK:IVCGF)) for €1.7 billion. The group will be able to satisfy the demand for basically all ground defense vehicles, a sector that is estimated to represent 40% of the European future spending in armaments. Also in this case, the expertise of Leonardo in electronic warfare could represent a good synergy with Iveco Defence.

This strategic acquisition marks a significant step in Leonardo’s plan to strengthen its role as a leading, fully integrated Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) in the land defence domain. The acquisition further enhances the Group’s comprehensive portfolio of solutions for defence and security, covering both tracked and wheeled platforms.

(Source: Leonardo Press Release)

Valuation

The new updated guidance underlines what was said. The new orders are €1.25-€1.75 billion higher than the previous guidance. This result is probably due to ReArm Europe and represents very good news for the company.

The group net debt is also good news: according to the new guidance, it is projected to reach €1.1 billion, a promising trend considering it was €3.0 billion in June 2024.

Finally, another interesting result is Leonardo's revenue, which recorded a +11.7% compared to the first six months of 2024, and showcases the company’s strength and continuous growth thanks to its effective policies.

As for the valuation, I think a good comparison can be made using EV/EBITDA multiples of the major European defense companies. Rheinmetall has a multiple of 39.41x, BAE Systems (OTCPK:BAESF)(BASEY) 14.40x, Thales (OTCPK:THLEF)(OTCPK:THLLY) 17.97x, Safran (OTCPK:SAFRF)(OTCPK:SAFRY)15.17x and Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF)(OTCPK:EADSY) 14.03x.

Leonardo has an EV/EBITDA multiple of 13.72x, which indicates that the company may be cheaper than the others. On the other hand, the current PE of 26.62 is currently higher than the historical average, indicating a possible overvaluation, but the forward P/E and PEG , respectively 20.33 and 1.47, may indicate that future growth can sustain current valuation.

By doing simple math on the multiples, considering the average EV/EBITDA of the sector, Leonardo could be valued around $65.50 and €48.30 for the stock traded in Milan.

Rating and Conclusion

In summary, Leonardo seems to be undervalued compared to the other European industries in the sector, indicating a possible undervaluation. On the other hand, there’s one thing that worries me about the current price, despite being undervalued on paper. The recent meeting between Trump and Putin may have signaled the investors a higher probability of ending the conflict in Ukraine. While this can be a good signal for the markets, it could have the opposite effect on the defense stocks. Although this remains only a hypothesis, it is a risk that the investors should be aware of given the current geopolitical conditions.

That being said, the stock is still a very good pick. The defense spending in Europe is far from over, even in the case of a possible ceasefire in Ukraine. Moreover, Leonardo has been able to position itself in future high-demand sectors. Therefore, considering all the growth opportunities this company can offer in the current landscape, I still consider it a buying opportunity.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Ranking Of Net-Lease REITs By Investment Spread For Q2 2025
data.description:
Introduction

In May, we published a ranking of investment-grade, net-lease REITs that are part of our coverage universe. The May report incorporated the Q1 operating performance of the respective REITs and, in this report, we incorporate Q2 performance information. Readers who periodically rebalance their exposure to particular net-lease REITs may find value in this review.

As with other asset classes, REIT valuation and profitability often move in cycles. For real estate assets, there is the space market cycle, which has to do with the underlying supply and demand for a particular type of space, and there is the capital market cycle, which has to do with the valuations at which REITs trade given the current macroeconomic environment. This ranking has to do with the latter.

Source: Ralp L. Block, Investing in REITs, 4th Edition, John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 2012, Hoboken, N.J.

Also in May, we published a bullish article on Global Net Lease, Inc. (GNL) and, on June 26th, S&P raised the issuer credit rating on GNL by one notch to BB+ and simultaneously raised the issue-level rating on their senior unsecured notes to BBB-. Notwithstanding the positive development, GNL still has not secured an issuer-level investment-grade credit rating from any of the three major credit rating agencies and is thus not included here. Another item we want to point out is that we anticipate publishing an article on First Industrial Realty Trust, Inc. (FR) in the near future. We have completed substantially all the work on First Industrial and are comfortable in our understanding of their business to include their stock in the list. First Industrial is an investment-grade net-lease REIT that focuses on industrial real estate assets.

Source: First Industrial 10-K 2024, Moody’s, Fitch

Investor Takeaway

Early in Q2, the tariff announcement by the White House introduced volatility to the financial markets that has subsequently waned in its effect. While many of the REITs covered in this article suggested there is still uncertainty looming over the broader economy, they have not seen material disruptions to their own or their tenants' businesses. Agree Realty Corporation (ADC), Essential Properties Realty Trust, Inc. (EPRT), NNN REIT, Inc. (NNN), Realty Income Corporation (O), and W. P. Carey Inc. (WPC) all increased investment volume for the year and raised guidance. STAG Industrial, Inc. (STAG), Gaming and Leisure Properties, Inc. (GLPI), and VICI Properties Inc. (VICI) raised either core FFO per share or AFFO per share. First Industrial Realty Trust, Inc. (FR) narrowed the range of their guidance and maintained the midpoint of the guided FFO for 2025.

The CEO of W.P. Carey seems to have captured the prevailing sentiment in the recent earnings call.

“the market feels quite strong, constructive right now. There are lots of opportunities. I think some of this is driven by what feels like a relatively stable interest rate environment. It's probably been the case now for several quarters, which is always a key ingredient in kind of the increase in transaction activity. So that's despite changing expectations throughout the year around tariffs. If you look at treasury yields, they've been mostly range bound in the low to mid-4s for most of this year, even dating back to the fourth quarter of last year. So that creates stability. I think you see transaction activity increase…”

The key lesson that the market keeps teaching us is that it is monetary policy (interest rates) and not fiscal policy (tariffs) that drives net-lease REITs’ operating performance. With the Fed projected to cut interest rates twice this year and major institutional players like Morgan Stanley, J.P. Morgan and others introducing or expanding their net-lease fund offerings, it is not too much of a stretch to surmise that net-lease REIT share prices should be buoyant for some time.

Two specific net-lease REITs that offer investors the best balance of risk, reward, investment spread and total return are Agree Realty and Realty Income.

Let us now turn to the details of our analysis.

Investment Spreads

Please see Table 1 for the net-lease REITs that we will evaluate.

Table 1: Investment Grade, Net-lease REITs
Ticker Sector Long-term Issuer Rating Outlook Issuing Agency
ADC Free Standing BBB+ Stable S&P
EPRT Free Standing BBB- Positive S&P
FR Industrial BBB Stable S&P
GLPI Gaming BBB- Stable Fitch
NNN Free Standing BBB+ Stable S&P
O Free Standing A- Stable S&P
STAG Industrial BBB Stable Fitch
VICI Gaming BBB- Stable S&P
WPC Diversified BBB+ Stable S&P

Source: Fitch, S&P, and Nareit REITWatch

There are two salient features that inform our analysis of net-lease REITs in this article. The first is that REITs, by design, cannot retain a significant amount of earnings. They must pay at least 90% of their taxable income without regard to dividend payments in order to maintain their REIT status. This means that for the REITs to grow revenue above the modest escalation that is included in their agreements with tenants, they must access debt and equity markets for capital and deploy that capital at a rate of return above the cost of debt and equity capital.

The second feature is that in a net-lease arrangement the tenant pays for all property-related costs. This includes real estate taxes, insurance, property operating expenses and upkeep. In substance, net-lease REITs are spread investors. They access debt and equity markets for capital, then acquire real estate and lease it to tenants in a net-lease arrangement. The rental income should cover the cost of equity and debt financing. Net-lease REITs have an economic profit that is the difference between the cost of acquisition of the real estate and the rental income derived from the acquired real estate. Therefore, to measure the health of a net-lease REIT at a particular point in time, we must evaluate this spread. The spread can be measured as the difference between the capitalization rate, or cap rate, and the cost of capital. The cap rate is frequently the net operating income of the real estate asset divided by the asset value. The cost of capital is the cost of equity and debt financing in proportion to how much of each is used in the business. The wider the spread, the more economic profit is being generated by the REIT.

Let us now turn to measuring the investment spreads of our REITs.

Capitalization Rate

REITs will have different cap rates because of the idiosyncrasies of their business model. Industrial net-lease REITs will have different cap rates than free-standing retail net-lease REITs. Even within a particular sector, there may be different cap rates depending on the relative size of the net-lease REIT and their bargaining power relative to their tenants. Whatever the determining factor may be, the cap rate is a reflection of the rate of return that the REIT can achieve in the current environment. For a business that is precluded from retaining capital, the cap rate must be higher than the cost of securing equity and debt financing for the investment to be accretive.

Let us take a look at the cap rates that the REITs achieved in Q2 2025. Please see Table 2.

Table 2: Net-lease REIT Cap Rates/Cash Yields
Ticker Recent Cap Rate/Cash Yield
ADC 7.10%
EPRT 7.90%
FR 6.40%
GLPI 7.75%
NNN 7.40%
O 7.50%
STAG 7.10%
VICI 7.30%
WPC 7.50%

Source: First Industrial Realty Q2 Earnings Supplement, VICI Properties Q1 2025 Earnings Supplement, STAG Industrial Press Release for Q2 2025, Press Releases and Earnings Transcripts already referenced above.

REITs don’t always measure cap rates the same way and often use a cash yield as a proxy for the cap rate. Also, sometimes, a REIT does not engage in acquiring real estate assets in the quarter. First Industrial did not report any acquisition activity in Q2 but did report one in Q1. In this case, we take the Q1 cap rate as an approximation of the cap rates that they can achieve in today’s economic environment. Another matter is reflected in the case of VICI and GLPI. Both did not report fee simple investments recently but have loan investments that could lead to a fee simple investment down the line. Investors should bear in mind that a fee simple investment in real estate is of a different character than an investment in a loan. Both may earn a similar rate of return, but a loan investment typically has a shorter duration, which means the capital would have to be redeployed again in a relatively shorter time horizon. A fee simple investment in real estate can be leased to tenants for decades.

Cost of Debt

The cost of debt is simply the interest rate at which the REIT can borrow funds. In general, the stronger the credit of the REIT, the lower the cost of borrowing. Please see Table 3.

Table 3: Median Yield on 10-year Tenor
Ticker Long-term Issuer Rating Median Yield
ADC BBB+ 5.21%
EPRT BBB- 5.21%
FR BBB 5.21%
GLPI BBB- 5.21%
NNN BBB+ 5.21%
O A- 4.91%
STAG BBB 5.21%
VICI BBB- 5.21%
WPC BBB+ 5.21%

Source: Fidelity

Unsurprisingly, Realty Income has the lowest cost of debt. Even at 4.91%, Realty Income can probably secure borrowing at a lower actual rate because of their portfolio exposure to Europe which gives them access to the lower interest rates available in Europe. W.P. Carey also has exposure to Europe and benefits from this same dynamic. Finally, Agree Realty would probably have a lower cost of debt than what is on the table in light of their recent upgrade from Fitch. If the reader would like to make some adjustments to the figures, we would suggest considering Realty Income as having the lowest cost of debt, followed by W.P. Carey, then Agree and then everyone else at 5.21%.

Cost of Equity

Now that we have evaluated the cost of debt, let us turn to the cost of equity. For REITs to attract investor capital, they need to offer a compelling rate of return. What rate should they offer? This question does not have a straightforward answer. For our purposes, we use three inputs. We start with the current U.S. 10-year Treasury as a proxy for the risk-free rate. For REITs to attract capital, they must offer a return above the risk-free rate. We then add a risk premium to the risk-free rate. Aswath Damodaran at NYU estimates that the current equity risk premium is at 3.84%. This estimate is well within the long-term average of between 3-5%. Finally, we incorporate the beta statistic of the stock. The beta statistic is a measure of how volatile the stock is relative to the market. A stock that is more volatile will have a beta statistic above 1.0, which has the effect of increasing the return demanded by investors. A stock that has a beta statistic of less than 1.0 will have the effect of reducing the return demanded by investors because of the muted volatility relative to the market. The market is assumed to have a beta statistic of 1.0.

Please see Table 4.

Table 4: Net Lease REITs
Ticker U.S. 10-year Treasury Equity Risk Premium β Cost of Equity
ADC 4.27% 3.84% 0.55 6.39%
EPRT 4.27% 3.84% 1.09 8.46%
FR 4.27% 3.84% 1.05 8.31%
GLPI 4.27% 3.84% 0.72 7.04%
NNN 4.27% 3.84% 0.93 7.84%
O 4.27% 3.84% 0.77 7.23%
STAG 4.27% 3.84% 0.91 7.77%
VICI 4.27% 3.84% 0.71 7.00%
WPC 4.27% 3.84% 0.79 7.31%

Source: Seeking Alpha and CNBC.

Given that a REIT’s revenue stream is contractual, it shouldn’t be surprising to find that a majority of REITs trade at a lower beta than the market. Notable exceptions to this generalization are experiential REITs like EPRT whose tenants operate in the consumer discretionary sector. First Industrial also has a beta statistic above 1.0, which could be a reflection of their business model. First Industrial does engage in some speculative development i.e., developing an asset without a tenant in hand. Agree Realty does not.

In Table 5, we have the respective weights of the different forms of capital in the particular REIT's capital structure. As an example, if Realty Income were to engage in an acquisition, they would likely finance the acquisition at about a 64% to 36% split between equity and debt financing.

Table 5: Capital Structure
Ticker Weight of Debt Weight of Equity Weight of Preferred
ADC 28.3% 70.2% 1.53%
EPRT 26.7% 73.3% 0.00%
FR 26.8% 73.2% 0.00%
GLPI 34.1% 65.9% 0.00%
NNN 36.7% 63.3% 0.00%
O 35.7% 64.3% 0.00%
STAG 31.0% 69.0% 0.00%
VICI 33.1% 66.9% 0.00%
WPC 38.1% 61.9% 0.00%

Source: Seeking Alpha, Q2 2025 Earnings Supplement for EPRT, First Industrial, Realty Income, VICI, W.P. Carey, Agree Realty 10-K for 2024

In Table 6, we have our cost of acquisition or formally, our weighted average cost of capital (WACC).

Table 6: Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)
Ticker Weight of Debt Cost of Debt Weight of Equity Cost of Equity Weight of Preferred Cost of Preferred WACC
ADC 28.3% 5.21% 70.2% 6.4% 1.5% 5.9% 6.04%
EPRT 26.7% 5.21% 73.3% 8.5% 0.0% 0.0% 7.59%
FR 26.8% 5.21% 73.2% 8.3% 0.0% 0.0% 7.48%
GLPI 34.1% 5.21% 65.9% 7.0% 0.0% 0.0% 6.42%
NNN 36.7% 5.21% 63.3% 7.8% 0.0% 0.0% 6.88%

Cost of Equity Revisited
