data.title: Top Preferred Stock, Investment Grade Issuer: Global Net Lease
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

"Don't throw the baby out with the bathwater!"

This well-known idiomatic expression warns against making a completely avoidable mistake – discarding something valuable while trying to eliminate something unwanted. For many, the idea of tossing out the baby along with the bathwater seems absolutely foolish. Yet, as reactive individuals, we frequently make poor choices, reacting to something small that causes us to lose the benefit of something larger.

When it comes to the stock market, many investors will eschew all levels of a company's capital stack simply because of the risk level or perceived riskiness of the common equity investment level. If you look at a company and see that its common equity is weak or underperforming, and then write off the entire company, you're essentially throwing out the baby with the bathwater. You're discarding potentially better investment opportunities higher up in the capital stack, along with the troubled common equity. When it comes to REITs, MLPs or even closed-end funds, there can be wonderful options that are not the common equity that should be investigated and researched further. This is one benefit we provide to members of High Dividend Opportunities: We refuse to reject a company at all levels because of one level.

Today, I want to look at a company that's flagged as being something to utterly avoid by esteemed writers here on Seeking Alpha – they threw out the baby with the bathwater and missed something even better higher up in the capital stack, at least when it comes to our style of investing.

As a reminder to my readers, I'm not a day trader, a momentum swing trader, or a commodities trader. No, no, I'm a dedicated long-term buy-and-hold style income investor, and as such, the vast majority of the returns I seek from the market are simply wonderful cash-in-hand dividends and distributions which provide me with the capital that I need to reinvest, to enjoy my day-to-day life, and to live in glorious comfort and ease.

Let's dive in!

Climbing The Capital Stack To Success

Global Net Lease (NYSE:GNL) is a net-lease REIT that owns and operates 1,200 properties in 10 countries, with 70% of its SLR (Straight-Line Rent) from the US and Canada, and ~30% from Europe. Approximately 60% of GNL’s SLR is derived from investment-grade tenants, and the REIT’s portfolio had a weighted average lease term of 6.2 years at the end of Q2 2025. About 88% of GNL’s leases carry contractual rent increases, providing natural inflation protection. GNL maintains a balanced industry exposure, with ~47% in industrial and distribution, 26% in retail, and around 27% in office properties, as measured by annualized SLR. No single tenant accounts for more than 5% of total SLR, and GNL’s top 10 tenants collectively contribute only 28% of total straight-line rent. Source

Office properties may cause some anxiety for readers, so we will note that GNL’s office portfolio maintained 100% rent collection from all tenants during the second quarter and has 77% of its tenants having investment-grade ratings. About 95% of GNL’s office portfolio is leased, and the bulk of the tenants are organizations that have imposed strict return-to-office mandates.

Earlier this month, GNL reported a transformative quarter with debt reduction as a core focus. The REIT reduced net debt by $2 billion over the past year, bringing down its net debt to adjusted EBITDA to 6.6x (from 8.1x). The company refinanced its $1.8 billion loan, pushing the due date back from 2026 to 2030, achieved slightly better interest rates, and gained more flexibility with their cash. This change positions the REIT to save $2 million in annual interest expenses, and there's no debt coming due until 2027.

During Q2, GNL completed the $1.8 billion sale of its multi-tenant retail portfolio to RCG Ventures, bringing total asset sales to $3 billion. This positions the REIT to focus on becoming a pure-play single-tenant net lease company with a high-quality portfolio. GNL used the proceeds from asset sales towards debt reduction, and the REIT repurchased 7.7 million shares for $56.5 million during Q2, bringing the total to 10.2 million shares for $76.9 million (since the announcement of the share repurchase program in February).

At the end of Q2, 85% of the REIT’s debt was fixed-rate, with the total debt carrying a weighted average interest rate of 4.3%. GNL maintains $1 billion in liquidity, and the REIT reported a 2.7x interest coverage ratio. Its weighted average debt maturity term increased to 3.7 years after the debt refinancing. This was a transformative quarter, with improved liquidity, reduced debt, and a strengthening credit profile. S&P Global upgraded the credit rating on GNL’s unsecured notes to investment-grade BBB-.

We have been discussing GNL preferreds quite frequently as discounted fixed-income securities from a REIT that is digesting a substantial merger with the Necessity Retail REIT in 2023. We insisted that readers stay away from the common stock as the dividend wasn’t safe, and we were right on point – the REIT had to cut its dividend three times since the completion of the merger. Yet, we insisted that the preferreds enjoyed strong safety and that they were going to be trading post call dates for the foreseeable future.

The strong Q2 report has had a positive impact on GNL common stock, and the sentiment (particularly the improved balance sheet health and credit ratings) has reflected on the preferreds as well. We see them much closer to par than seen in recent years, but they are still attractive, reflective of the relative strength in the underlying business. GNL preferreds currently offer up to 7.8% yield and modest upside to par:

  • 7.25% Series A, Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Stock (GNL.PR.A) - Yield 7.8%
  • 6.875% Series B, Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Stock (GNL.PR.B) - Yield 7.6%
  • 7.50% Series D, Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Stock (GNL.PR.D) - Yield 7.8%
  • 7.375% Series E, Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Stock (GNL.PR.E) - Yield 7.8%

For 1H 2025, GNL spent $21 million on preferred stock dividends, which were well covered by $119 million in AFFO for the period. We reiterate that GNL maintains $144.8 million in cash and cash equivalents, and a total liquidity of $1 billion, providing adequate safety for the preferreds as GNL continues to streamline its operations.

Conclusion

A dividend-paying business facing headwinds is often said to be in the "danger zone." However, headwinds are a natural part of the business cycle, and companies often emerge stronger through strategic changes. In this case, the GNL-RTL merger has had a negative impact on common shareholders as they have suffered three dividend cuts since then. But don't throw the baby out with the bath water.

Ring up the S&P Global, as they have deemed GNL's debt investment grade.

As an income investor, I seek high-yielding income from a diverse range of sources. To do so, I'm willing to look deeper than a basic evaluation of common equity, dig deeper than a five-year chart, and adopt a contrarian position against the prevailing sentiment of traders.

When it comes to retirement, you don't get the excuse of blaming others for your successes or failures. You are responsible for your financial position and future. Using my unique Income Method, you can revolutionize your retirement, radically alter your outlook, and reveal a new perspective on investing.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Energy Price As An Economic Indicator
data.description:
What are energy prices telling us about the economy? A recent article on Bloomberg noted that:

“Hedge funds slashed their bullish position on crude to the lowest in about 17 years as risks of additional sanctions on Russian crude oil waned, bringing concerns about a global supply glut back to the fore. Money managers’ net-long position on West Texas Intermediate shrank by 19,578 lots to 29,686 lots in the week ended Tuesday, data from the Commodity Futures Trading Commission show. That’s the lowest since October 2008.”

However, there is more to this story than just easing global tensions and rising oil supplies impacting energy prices. Energy prices indicate economic strength, or, in this case, weakness. If the global economy grew strongly, the need for oil consumption would rise, absorbing the current production levels causing energy prices to rise. However, the outlook for economic growth in the major oil consumption economies, the U.S. and the Eurozone, is very weak.

This is also why we are seeing the fastest pace of Central Bank rate cuts since the pandemic crisis in 2020, and the Federal Reserve in the U.S. is now joining in despite record-high stock markets.

However, I would be remiss in not stating that energy prices are susceptible to "geopolitical shocks." This is because NYMEX traders bid up oil prices in anticipation of oil shortages, or conversely, excesses, but these price fluctuations tend to be shorter-term events.

Over the longer term, energy prices are an indicator of economic strength or weakness. This is because, as noted previously, energy is consumed in every part of the economic cycle.

“High oil prices add to the costs of doing business which pass, ultimately, on to customers and businesses. Whether it is higher cab fares, more expensive airline tickets, the cost of apples shipped from California, or new furniture shipped from China, high oil prices can result in higher prices for seemingly unrelated products and services.” – Investopedia

Of course, consumers who fill up their gas tanks each week immediately notice high oil prices. While core inflation reports strip out food and energy, those items drive short-term consumption patterns. Given that consumption comprises roughly 70% of the GDP calculation, the impact of higher oil prices is almost immediate.

As shown above, spikes in oil prices are highly correlated with economic recessions, financial events, and oil price reversions. Therefore, let’s explore energy prices as an economic indicator to see what oil is currently telling us about the state of the U.S. economy.

The Link To Oil

As noted, energy prices are crucial to the overall economic equation. As prices increase, consumers face higher inflationary costs. Unsurprisingly, there is a high correlation between the rise and fall of energy prices and the consumer price index. Unsurprisingly, energy prices and inflation have declined as economic demand weakened following the 2020-2021 stimulus-driven economic growth surge.

Since energy prices feed into virtually every aspect of our lives, from our food to the products and services we buy, we can expand our view of oil as an economic indicator. Therefore, the demand side of the equation is a tell-tale sign of economic strength or weakness. The chart below uses an economic composite of GDP, interest rates, and inflation compared to oil prices. It would be unsurprising that there is a decent correlation between the two.

Since the oil industry is manufacturing and production-intensive, falling energy prices impact other economically important facets of manufacturing, employment, and capital expenditures. The chart below shows oil prices and events relative to the deviation from the 4-year average energy price. While geopolitical events spike energy prices, as in 2022, those spikes result in economic downturns, reducing demand, thereby lowering energy prices.

While the pandemic-driven shutdown of the economy created a supply shortage, the flood of liquidity inevitably created a demand surge. That “pull-forward” of consumption led to surging inflationary pressures and rising oil prices. We show the high correlation between oil prices and breakeven inflation rates.

Oil prices reflect overall economic activity, given their vast contribution to everything consumed. With liquidity reversing, economic demand is weakening as the cost of living outpaces real wages. As such, the correlation between declining energy price, future economic growth, and inflation breakevens should be unsurprising.

The Fed Should Pay Closer Attention To The Energy Price

As noted above, global central banks have been cutting interest rates at the fastest pace since the pandemic, as many of those countries teeter on the edge of recession. However, the Federal Reserve remained steadfast until recently that its concern was a resurgence of inflation due to tariffs. But, as noted above, energy prices warned that inflation is not a concern for the U.S., and the Fed may be behind the curve in cutting rates.

Given that consumption makes up ~70% of the GDP calculation and the importance of oil in everything we consume, it should be unsurprising that oil prices also reflect economic activity in the aggregate. The current decline in energy prices says a lot about underlying economic strength, which the Fed noted has shown up in the latest employment data.

“In the near term, risks to inflation are tilted to the upside, and risks to employment to the downside—a challenging situation. When our goals are in tension like this, our framework calls for us to balance both sides of our dual mandate.” – J. Powell

However, as noted above, energy prices clearly show that inflation risks are tilted to the downside, with the larger risk being a much weaker economic growth rate. As such, the Federal Reserve should likely cut rates more aggressively now to avoid the potential warning from the decline in oil prices.

If energy prices reflect economic activity, then their current trend points to growing weakness beneath the surface of headline data. While equity markets reach new highs and unemployment appears low, the sharp drop in oil prices and declining breakeven inflation expectations tell a different story. It’s not geopolitics or supply excesses driving prices lower. It’s waning demand. And that’s a red flag.

The Fed’s delayed response to this signal is concerning. As Powell notes, their framework must balance inflation and employment. But right now, inflation pressures are easing, not accelerating. Oil prices reflect that shift more clearly than any lagging indicator. If consumption is slowing, and energy prices strongly suggest it is, then the Fed should move faster, not slower. Waiting for confirmation in backward-looking data risks compounding economic weakness already in motion.

Historically, when oil prices fall significantly, the economy follows. The Fed would do well to pay attention.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
